mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a robust economic expansion, leading to higher expected corporate earnings and positive investor sentiment. This effect is persistent and drives near-term equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS has shown consistent positive growth through 2006.
failure_condition: The signal's walk-forward Information Coefficient (IC) remains below 0.02 for a rolling 3-year period, indicating no predictive power of payroll growth for 20-day returns.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp year-over-year decline in housing starts (HOUST) signals a contraction in a key cyclical sector, presaging broader economic weakness and lower corporate profits, leading to negative medium-term equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts have fallen significantly from their 2006 peak, with the latest reading at 1588K well below the prior year.
failure_condition: In periods where the trigger condition is met, S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive more than 50% of the time.

mechanism_narrative: An elevated Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) increases the discount rate for future earnings and makes fixed income alternatives more attractive than equities, creating a persistent headwind for stock prices.
evidence_refs: fact:The Fed has raised rates to 5.24% and held them there, indicating a restrictive policy stance.
failure_condition: When FEDFUNDS is above 4.5%, the average 20-day S&P 500 return is not statistically significantly lower than when it is below 4.5% (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Decelerating industrial production (INDPRO) indicates a slowdown in the manufacturing and production sectors, which are leading indicators of corporate revenue and earnings weakness, negatively impacting equity valuations.
evidence_refs: fact:Recent INDPRO readings show a flattening trend after a period of growth.
failure_condition: The correlation between 3-month changes in INDPRO and 60-day forward S&P 500 returns is positive over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Strong momentum in retail sales (RSAFS) indicates robust consumer spending, which constitutes a large portion of GDP. This strength translates directly into higher revenues for consumer-facing companies, boosting short-term market performance.
evidence_refs: fact:Retail sales have continued to trend upwards, suggesting consumer resilience.
failure_condition: A strategy going long the S&P 500 for 5 days following a positive monthly RSAFS surprise underperforms a buy-and-hold strategy over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: When monetary policy is restrictive (high FEDFUNDS), a positive inflation shock (rising CPIAUCSL) increases the probability of further Fed tightening, raising discount rates and recession risk, which negatively impacts equities.
evidence_refs: fact:The Fed is in a tightening cycle while inflation remains a concern.
failure_condition: In periods of high FEDFUNDS, the correlation between monthly changes in CPIAUCSL and 60-day forward S&P 500 returns is non-negative.

mechanism_narrative: A very low unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a tight labor market, which leads to wage-push inflation and compresses corporate profit margins. This is a late-cycle indicator that precedes market downturns.
evidence_refs: fact:The unemployment rate is at a cyclical low of 4.5%.
failure_condition: Periods where UNRATE is below 4.6% are not followed by lower average 60-day S&P 500 returns compared to periods where UNRATE is above 4.6%.

mechanism_narrative: A weakening housing market (falling HOUST) combined with weakening consumer spending (falling RSAFS) creates a powerful negative feedback loop, signaling a broad-based demand slowdown and imminent recession, causing negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing is already in a downturn; any concurrent weakness in retail sales would be a strong negative signal.
failure_condition: When both HOUST and RSAFS are declining, the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 50% of instances.
